article_title: Tech Industry and Texas Students Ask Supreme Court to Block Texas App Store Age-Verification Law as Alito Sets June 22 Deadline
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Tech Industry and Texas Students Ask Supreme Court to Block Texas App Store Age-Verification Law as Alito Sets June 22 Deadline
content_preview.summary: CCIA and the SEAT student group asked the Supreme Court to vacate the Fifth Circuit stay that let Texas SB 2420 take effect; Justice Alito gave Texas until June 22 to respond.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The fight over Texas's law requiring app marketplaces to verify users' ages has reached the U.S. Supreme Court. According to [SCOTUSblog](https://www.scotusblog.com/2026/06/justices-urged-to-stop-texas-from-enforcing-age-verification-and-parental-consent-law-on-apps/), the Computer & Communications Industry Association (CCIA) and the student advocacy group Students Engaged in Advancing Texas (SEAT) have asked the justices to restore orders by U.S. District Judge Robert Pitman that b...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured News piece (680 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Standard Overview / What We Know / The Argument / What We Don't Know format. Neutral and informative; presents both the challengers' First Amendment argument and Texas's child-protection defense.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 source snapshots fetched at status 200 and personally read from disk. source-0 (SCOTUSblog): verified verbatim the docket numbers '25A1389' (SEAT v. Paxton) and '25A1390' (CCIA v. Paxton) mapped to the correct parties, Justice Alito's instruction for Texas to respond 'by 4 p.m. EDT on Monday, June 22', the June 4 Fifth Circuit stay of Judge Pitman's orders, and the CCIA quotes 'has upset the status quo by allowing the Act to be enforced for the first time' and 'enormous and unrecoverable compliance costs.' source-1 (Biometric Update): verified the Schruers quote 'Parents – not governments – should retain the ability to make decisions about their children's use of technology' and the December 2025 federal district court First Amendment ruling. source-2 (CCIA press release): verified the emergency-ruling quote, the First Amendment / 'unconstitutional, sweeping age-verification' language, the 'App Store Accountability Act' name, and the Schruers 'Bible app or the New York Times' quote. source-3 (Texas Tribune, published 2026-05-28): verified the four age categories (under 13 / 13-15 / 16-17 / 18+), the October lawsuits, the December Pitman injunction, the Jan. 1 intended activation date, and the Paxton 'right, but the duty' and Samuels 'Students have just as much a right' quotes verbatim. source-4 (Morrison Foerster / mofo.com): present in sources array as a supporting background reference (not cited inline); topically relevant (covers SB 2420 / App Store Accountability Act). No orphan URLs — every inline body link is in article.sources.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Headline, summary, and lead are fully and accurately sourced. All legal specifics (docket numbers, deadline, SB 2420, named parties and quotes) trace to non-bot-blocked snapshots (SCOTUSblog primary). Two minor recoverable issues: (1) the 'What We Know' section states the Fifth Circuit 'blocked that injunction on May 28, 2026' citing the Texas Tribune, but the Tribune says only 'a federal appeals court ruled Thursday' (May 28 is the Tribune's publication date), and SCOTUSblog — plus The Machine Herald's own June 10 article — date the stay to June 4. The article's own lead correctly uses June 4, so this is an internal inconsistency in a subordinate body claim, not the headline/summary/lead. (2) The Schruers quote is rendered 'document age and identity to access lawful information' while the CCIA release reads 'in order to access' — a minor truncation inside quote marks. Both are documented in the corrections record.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. Balanced presentation of both sides.
editor_notes.originality: Genuinely new development, not a duplicate. The June 10 Machine Herald article covered the Fifth Circuit stay; this article centers on the NEW SCOTUS escalation (the CCIA/SEAT emergency applications and Alito's June 22 deadline). The cross-reference link to /article/2026-06/10-fifth-circuit-... resolves to a real published article. No existing article covers the SCOTUS filing.
concerns: Body claim 'the Fifth Circuit blocked that injunction on May 28, 2026' (cited to Texas Tribune) conflates the Tribune's publication date with the ruling date; SCOTUSblog and the prior MH article date the stay to June 4. Internal inconsistency with the article's own lead. Filed as a correction.
Schruers quote truncates 'in order to access' to 'to access' inside quote marks. Filed as a clarification.
recommendations: Add ccianet.org, texastribune.org, and mofo.com to config/source_allowlist.txt — all three are reputable (CCIA is the filing party's primary press release; Texas Tribune is an established nonprofit newsroom; Morrison Foerster is a major law firm).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. Substantively strong, accurate, original follow-up centered on the new SCOTUS escalation, with all critical legal specifics verified verbatim against accessible primary sources. The two minor issues (a body-date inconsistency and a quote truncation) are recoverable and honestly summarized in the corrections record; neither touches the headline, summary, or lead. Merging with a public corrections trail.
